probable_causes: Insulation aging and embrittlement from thermal cycling
Partial discharge activity in end-winding region
Contamination from moisture, oil, or dust ingress
Mechanical looseness of stator bars in slots
diagnostic_procedures: Perform insulation resistance and polarization index (PI) test
Conduct partial discharge measurement — online or offline
Test tan-delta at various voltage steps for tip-up analysis
Visually inspect end-windings for contamination, loose ties, or tracking
corrective_actions: Re-wedge loose stator bars in slots
Clean and re-varnish contaminated winding sections
Replace severely damaged stator bar coils
Tighten or replace end-winding support ties and blocks
preventive_actions: Schedule annual offline insulation testing during planned outage
Install online partial discharge monitoring system
Maintain generator enclosure sealing to prevent contamination
Record and trend insulation test results over the years
safety_precautions: High voltage testing — follow strict HV safety procedures
Ensure generator is de-energized, grounded, and isolated before any winding work
Hydrogen must be fully purged before opening generator casing

probable_causes: Hydrogen side seal drain not functioning properly
Shaft surface finish deterioration at seal area
diagnostic_procedures: Monitor hydrogen purity trend — decreasing purity indicates seal leak
Check seal oil differential pressure across hydrogen and air side
Inspect seal drain sight glasses for abnormal flow
corrective_actions: Replace worn seal rings during major overhaul
Restore shaft surface finish at seal contact area
Adjust seal oil system pressure regulators
Repair or replace seal oil drain system components
preventive_actions: Monitor hydrogen purity and makeup rate daily
Maintain seal oil quality — filtration and moisture control
Plan seal ring replacement during scheduled overhauls
safety_precautions: Hydrogen is explosive — strictly no smoking or open flames near generator
Follow hydrogen handling and purging procedures meticulously
Ensure gas detection alarms are operational

common_failures.failure: Excitation system or brush gear problems
probable_causes: Brush wear or incorrect spring pressure
Slip ring surface roughness or eccentricity
diagnostic_procedures: Inspect brush condition — length, contact area, sparking level
Measure slip ring runout and surface finish
Test AVR control cards and thyristor firing circuits
Perform exciter field winding insulation resistance test
corrective_actions: Replace brushes and adjust spring pressure to OEM specification
Machine slip rings to restore surface finish if runout exceeds limits
Repair exciter winding insulation or re-wind if necessary
preventive_actions: Schedule regular brush inspection and replacement
Track slip ring runout measurements during outages
Keep spare AVR cards and exciter components in stock
safety_precautions: Carbon dust from brushes is conductive — maintain cleanliness
Brush replacement on running machine requires trained personnel only
